timeline.event: Born in Edinburgh, Scotland
Mother died (age 8)
Produced his first scientific work, a paper on 'Oval Curves', at age 14
Enrolled at the University of Edinburgh
Began studying at the University of Cambridge
Graduated from Cambridge as Second Wrangler in the Mathematical Tripos and first in the Smith's Prize exam
Elected as a Fellow of Trinity College to supervise experiments on electricity and magnetism and research color theory
Published a paper on the structure of Saturn's rings
Married Katherine Mary Dewar
Awarded the Adams Prize for his research on Saturn's rings
Appointed Professor of Natural Philosophy and Astronomy at King's College London, and received the Rumford Medal from the Royal Society of London for his research on color vision
Produced the first color photograph by applying the mixing of the three primary colors
Published 'A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field'
Resigned from his professorship at King's College and became a professor at Cambridge University
Statistically formulated the kinetic theory of gases
Researched at the Cavendish Laboratory
Published 'A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism'
Began editing Henry Cavendish's unpublished electrical experiment data
Died of stomach cancer in Cambridge at the age of 48 on November 5

keyAchievements.title: Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Laid a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena, established electromagnetism, and proved that light is an electromagnetic wave.

keyAchievements.title: Maxwell's equations
keyAchievements.description: Mathematically organized existing electromagnetic theories and established Maxwell's equations, the differential equations that form the basis of electromagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Kinetic theory of gases
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the velocity distribution law of gas molecules and introduced probabilistic concepts, laying the foundation for statistical mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Color theory and color photography
keyAchievements.description: Produced the first color photograph in 1861 by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Saturn's rings
keyAchievements.description: Showed that Saturn's rings must be composed of small particles to be stable, a prediction proven by the Voyager spacecraft in the 1980s.

faq.question: Who was James Clerk Maxwell?
faq.answer: A British theoretical physicist and mathematician born in Edinburgh, Scotland, recognized as the founder of electromagnetism for establishing a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena.

faq.question: What is Maxwell's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Laying a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena and establishing electromagnetism, as well as presenting 'Maxwell's equations' that mathematically structured this.

faq.question: What contribution did Maxwell make to color photography?
faq.answer: In 1861, he created the first color photograph by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

faq.question: When and where was Maxwell born, and when did he die?
faq.answer: Born on June 13, 1831, in Edinburgh, Scotland, and died of stomach cancer in Cambridge on November 5, 1879, at the age of 48.

timeline.event: Zenobia, a Palmyrene noblewoman, married King Odaenathus
Odaenathus was assassinated, and Zenobia became the regent of the Palmyrene Empire in place of her young son Vaballathus
Zenobia successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt
Declared Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and herself as Empress of Palmyra
Defeated by Roman Emperor Aurelian and taken to Rome as a captive, bringing the Palmyrene Empire to ruin
자브다스 장군이 이끄는 팔미라 제국 군대가 이집트와 시리아를 정복함.
팔미라 제국 군대가 이집트 총독을 살해함.
팔미라 제국이 아나톨리아와 팔레스타인을 정복하며 영토를 확장함.
로마 제국의 아우렐리아누스 황제가 팔미라 제국 정복을 결심하고 군대를 이끌고 침공함.
로마 제국이 팔미라 제국을 정복함.
로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에서 포로로 끌려옴.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Palmyrene Empire
keyAchievements.description: After her husband's death, she ruled the Palmyrene Empire as regent and expanded its territory by conquering Roman Egypt.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of an independent state
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to establish an independent state from the Roman Empire by capturing large areas of Roman territory, including the Levant and Asia Minor.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Imperial titles
keyAchievements.description: Declared her son Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and elevated herself to Empress of Palmyra.

faq.question: Who was Zenobia?
faq.answer: A noblewoman and ruler of the Palmyrene Empire, who acted as regent after her husband Odaenathus was assassinated, bringing the empire to its peak before clashing with the Roman Empire and eventually leading to its downfall.

faq.question: What territories did Zenobia conquer?
faq.answer: She successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt, the Levant, and Asia Minor.

faq.question: How did Zenobia's rule come to an end?
faq.answer: She was defeated by the newly ascended Roman Emperor Aurelian, taken to Rome as a captive, and the Palmyrene Empire fell.

faq.question: 제노비아는 어떻게 팔미라 제국의 통치자가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 267년 오데나투스 황제 암살 후 아들 바발라투스를 후계자로 임명하며 팔미라 제국의 공동 통치자가 되었습니다.

faq.question: 제노비아의 최후는 어떻게 되었나요?
faq.answer: 274년 로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에 포로로 끌려왔으나, 그 이후의 정확한 생사는 불명확합니다.

timeline.event: Born in Portsmouth, England
Father John Dickens is imprisoned for debt; Charles begins working at a blacking factory
Starts career as a journalist
Publishes 'Sketches by Boz'
Gains explosive popularity with the serialization of 'The Pickwick Papers'; marries Catherine Hogarth
Publishes 'Oliver Twist'
First visit to the United States
Publishes 'A Christmas Carol'
Publishes 'David Copperfield'
Purchases Gad's Hill Place, a house he admired since childhood
Separates from his wife Catherine
Publishes 'A Tale of Two Cities'
Publishes 'Great Expectations'
Involved in the Staplehurst rail crash
Second visit to the United States and conducts reading tours
Dies of a stroke; buried in Poet's Corner at Westminster Abbey
《데이비드 코퍼필드》를 출판했다.
《A Child's History of England》를 출판했다.
《황폐한 집》을 출판했다.
《어려운 시절》을 출판했다.
《작은 도릿》을 출판했다.
《두 도시 이야기》를 출판했다.
《위대한 유산》을 출판했다.
《Our Mutual Friend》를 출판했다.
두 번째 미국 방문이 이루어졌다.
《에드윈 드루드의 비밀》을 출판했다.
58세의 나이로 개즈 힐에서 숨을 거두었다.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of memorable characters
keyAchievements.description: Created some of the world's most famous fictional characters, including Ebenezer Scrooge, Oliver Twist, and David Copperfield.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for social reform
keyAchievements.description: Strongly criticized the social contradictions, poverty, and child labor issues brought about by the Industrial Revolution through his novels, contributing to social reform.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of the serialization format
keyAchievements.description: Greatly contributed to the popularization of the novel serialization format by publishing novels in weekly or monthly installments, receiving tremendous response from readers.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Christmas
keyAchievements.description: Often referred to as 'The Man Who Invented Christmas,' his work 'A Christmas Carol' played a significant role in establishing the modern culture and concept of Christmas.

faq.question: What is Charles Dickens's most representative work?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'David Copperfield', 'A Tale of Two Cities', and 'Great Expectations'.

faq.question: What was Dickens's childhood like?
faq.answer: His father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a blacking factory at the age of 12. This harsh experience deeply influenced his later works.

faq.question: Why did Dickens's novels usually come out in serial form?
faq.answer: He published them in weekly or monthly magazines to make his works accessible to the public at an affordable price, and this serialization method became very popular during the Victorian era.

faq.question: Did Dickens's works actually influence society?
faq.answer: Yes, his vivid descriptions of the plight of the poor and the poor conditions of child labor raised public awareness and contributed to real social reforms.

faq.question: How did Dickens die?
faq.answer: He died of a stroke at his home, Gad's Hill Place, on June 9, 1870, at the age of 58.

timeline.event: Born in Marton, Yorkshire, England
Began working as an apprentice on a merchant ship
Volunteered for the Royal Navy
Promoted to ship's master; participated in the Seven Years' War and surveyed the St. Lawrence River in Canada
Married Elizabeth Batts
Commanded the HMS Endeavour on his first voyage, tasked with observing the transit of Venus and searching for the 'Unknown Southern Land' (Terra Australis)
Arrived in Tahiti and observed the transit of Venus
Explored the coastline of New Zealand and discovered the Cook Strait
Arrived on the eastern coast of Australia (Botany Bay) and claimed it for Britain
The Endeavour was damaged on the Great Barrier Reef, undergoing repairs
Returned to England, completing the first voyage
Commanded the HMS Resolution on his second voyage, circumnavigating the globe as far south as possible to search for the 'Unknown Southern Land'
Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time in history
Returned to England, completing the second voyage; proved that a vast habitable southern continent did not exist
Commanded the HMS Resolution on his third voyage to discover the Northwest Passage
Discovered the Hawaiian Islands and named them the 'Sandwich Islands'
Explored the western coast of North America and reached the Bering Strait but was blocked by ice
Returned to Hawaii, got into a conflict with the indigenous people, and was killed
힉스곶으로 명명했던 육지를 목격하며 오스트레일리아 대륙 동쪽 해안에 도달한 최초의 유럽인이 됨
오스트레일리아 대륙에 처음 상륙 (현재 커널반도)
그레이트 배리어 리프 북쪽에 인데버 호가 부딪혀 난파
포제션섬에 상륙하여 오스트레일리아 동해안의 영국 영유권을 선언
바타비아에서 말라리아와 이질로 많은 일행이 사망한 후 귀국
인데버호가 남 잉글랜드의 다운즈에 닻을 내리고 쿡은 켄트로 하선
유럽인으로서 처음으로 남극권에 돌입 (두 번째 항해)
통가, 이스터섬, 뉴칼레도니아, 바누아투에 도착 후 남아메리카 남단을 돌아 사우스 조지아 및 샌드위치 제도를 발견 (두 번째 항해)
하와이 제도를 방문한 최초의 유럽인이 됨 (세 번째 항해)
사망

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Australia and New Zealand
keyAchievements.description: Was the first European to encounter the eastern coastline of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and completed the first recorded circumnavigation of New Zealand.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of accurate maps
keyAchievements.description: Produced maps of unprecedented accuracy with his excellent seamanship and surveying skills, changing the shape of the world map.

keyAchievements.title: Scurvy prevention
keyAchievements.description: Significantly reduced the incidence of scurvy among his crew by enforcing strict hygiene and providing diets including foods like sauerkraut.

keyAchievements.title: Search for the Northwest Passage and Antarctica
keyAchievements.description: Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time on his second voyage, and explored the Bering Strait in search of the Northwest Passage on his third voyage.

keyAchievements.title: 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 뉴펀들랜드와 뉴질랜드의 해도를 제작했으며, 특히 뉴펀들랜드 해안의 대규모의 정확한 지도를 처음으로 만들었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 괴혈병 사망자 없는 세계 일주
keyAchievements.description: 첫 번째 항해에서 사상 처음으로 괴혈병에 의한 사망자를 내지 않고 세계 일주를 달성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 영국 식민지 개척 기여
keyAchievements.description: 태평양을 남쪽 끝에서부터 북쪽 끝까지 탐험하여 영국의 식민지 개척에 크게 이바지했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 대항해시대의 종결
keyAchievements.description: 소위 대항해시대의 끝을 장식한 인물로, 그의 탐험을 전후하여 세계의 거의 모든 지역이 유럽인에게 알려지게 되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 뉴질랜드 해안 지도 제작 및 해협 발견
keyAchievements.description: 뉴질랜드 해안의 거의 완벽한 지도를 제작하고 뉴질랜드 남북섬을 나누는 쿡 해협을 발견했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남극권 최초 돌입
keyAchievements.description: 유럽인으로서 처음으로 남극권에 돌입하는 위대한 업적을 달성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남방 대륙 탐험 종지부
keyAchievements.description: 남극 대륙을 발견하였지만, 남방 대륙이 인류가 거주 가능한 위도에는 존재하지 않는다는 것을 확인하여 전설의 남방 대륙 탐험에 종지부를 찍었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 정확한 경도 결정
keyAchievements.description: 두 번째 항해에서 크로노미터가 활약하여 정확한 경도를 결정하는 데 크게 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 북서부 해안 탐사 및 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 캘리포니아에서 베링 해협에 이르는 미국의 북서부 해안 대부분의 해도를 제작하고 알래스카의 곳곳을 파악했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 세계 일주 항해 일지 작성
keyAchievements.description: 자필 원고로 세계 일주 항해 일지를 남겼습니다(제2 항해).

faq.question: What is Captain James Cook's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: Leading three Pacific voyages, discovering the eastern coast of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and mapping the Pacific Ocean in great detail.

faq.question: Did James Cook discover Australia?
faq.answer: He was not the first European to discover Australia. Dutch explorers had reached the western coast earlier, but Cook was the first to explore and map the eastern coast.

faq.question: How did Captain Cook die?
faq.answer: During his third voyage in 1779, after returning to Hawaii, a conflict arose with the indigenous people over a stolen boat, and he was killed during the altercation.

faq.question: Did Captain Cook find the Northwest Passage?
faq.answer: No, he explored up to the Bering Strait but could not find a navigable Northwest Passage because he was blocked by ice.

faq.question: What was Captain Cook's relationship with the indigenous people like?
faq.answer: He generally tried to maintain peaceful relations, but there were also cultural misunderstandings and violent clashes, a legacy that remains controversial today.

faq.question: 그의 첫 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 왕립 학회는 금성 일면통과 관측을 위해 그를 남태평양에 파견했습니다.

faq.question: 그의 두 번째 항해의 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 남방 대륙(테라 아우스트랄리스)을 발견하기 위해 왕립 학회에 의해 다시 파견되었습니다.

faq.question: 그의 세 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 북극해를 빠져나와 태평양과 대서양을 잇는 북서 항로를 탐색하는 것이었습니다.

timeline.event: December 10: Emily Elizabeth Dickinson was born in Amherst, Massachusetts, USA.
May 15: Died at the age of fifty-five.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry Creation
keyAchievements.description: Wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

keyAchievements.title: Main Themes of Her Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Created poems with main themes including love, death, parting, the soul, and heaven.

keyAchievements.title: Lack of Public Recognition in Life
keyAchievements.description: Was largely unrecognized by the public during her lifetime, with only about 4 of her poems published in collections.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Exchanges in Life
keyAchievements.description: Consistently exchanged poems through correspondence with a few poets and intellectuals who recognized her talent during her lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Recognition of Genius
keyAchievements.description: Her genius was widely recognized after her death when her younger sister, Lavinia Norcross Dickinson, published her collections of poems.

keyAchievements.title: Musical Reinterpretation of Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Her poems have been set to music by renowned composers such as Aaron Copland.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson recognized by the public during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not recognized by the public during her lifetime, and only 4 of her poems were published.

faq.question: What themes did Emily Dickinson's poetry primarily deal with?
faq.answer: Her poetry mainly dealt with themes of love, death, separation, the soul, and heaven.

faq.question: How many poems did Emily Dickinson write?
faq.answer: She wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson a reclusive poet?
faq.answer: She is known as a reclusive poet who strictly refrained from going out, but as a child, she had a lively personality, going out to the fields every day and playing with neighborhood children.

timeline.event: Mary Ann Evans (George Eliot) was born.
Family moved to Griff House.
Mother died.
Completed English translation of Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus'.
Father died.
Traveled to Switzerland with the Brays.
Returned to England and moved to London.
Became assistant editor of 'The Westminster Review'.
Began contributing essays and reviews to 'The Westminster Review'.
Translated Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'.
Resigned from 'The Westminster Review'.
Published the novel 'Adam Bede'.
Published the novel 'The Mill on the Floss'.
Published the novel 'Silas Marner'.
Published the novel 'Romola'.
Published the novel 'Middlemarch'.
Published the novel 'Daniel Deronda'.
George Eliot died.

keyAchievements.title: Important Writer of the Victorian Era
keyAchievements.description: Worked as a novelist, poet, journalist, and translator, being one of the most important writers of the Victorian era.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Major Novels
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a total of 7 novels, including 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda', showcasing realism and psychological insight.

keyAchievements.title: Translation of Philosophical Books
keyAchievements.description: Translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' (1846) and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity' (1854).

keyAchievements.title: Editorial Work at 'The Westminster Review'
keyAchievements.description: Worked as the assistant editor of 'The Westminster Review' from 1851 to mid-1854, carrying out practical publishing tasks and contributing many essays and reviews.

keyAchievements.title: Overcoming Prejudice Through a Pen Name
keyAchievements.description: Used the pen name 'George Eliot' to escape prejudice against female writers and to have her works evaluated objectively.

faq.question: What was George Eliot's real name?
faq.answer: George Eliot's real name was Mary Ann Evans.

faq.question: What was the main reason George Eliot used a pen name?
faq.answer: To escape the prejudice that female writers only write lighthearted romance novels, to be evaluated separately from her role as an editor and critic, and to avoid scandals regarding her private life.

faq.question: What are George Eliot's representative novel works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda'.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of her novels?
faq.answer: Most of her novels are set in rural England and are famous for their realism and psychological insight.

faq.question: What major works did George Eliot translate?
faq.answer: She translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'.

timeline.event: Born at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.
Studied at the Reading Abbey Girls' School in Reading, Berkshire.
Started writing novels at the age of 14.
Wrote the epistolary novel 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility' based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Dedicated 'Emma' to the Prince Regent (later George IV) shortly before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, leaving her bedridden for long periods.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 while writing 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Birth (Estimated).
Death of her mother and younger brother William Aigret.
Death of her father William X; succeeded to the Duchy of Aquitaine and the County of Poitiers (age 15).
Married Louis VII.
Death of Louis VI.
Louis VII became King of France.
Affair between Raoul I and Petronilla began; vacancy of the Archbishopric of Bourges occurred.
War between Louis VII and Theobald II.
Louis and Eleanor visited the newly built Basilica of Saint-Denis.
Birth of her first daughter, Marie.
Pope Eugene III requested Louis to lead the Second Crusade.
Crusade assembly at Vézelay; Eleanor also decided to join.
Agreed to divorce Louis VII.
Marriage to Louis VII was annulled.
Married Henry Plantagenet.
Henry became King of England.
Coronation as Queen of England.
Introduced maritime law to Oleron.
Birth of her last child with Henry II.
Stayed in England to arrange the marriage of her daughter Matilda to Henry the Lion, Duke of Saxony; agreed to separate from Henry around Christmas and left for Poitiers.
Stayed in Poitiers.
Henry the Young King led a rebellion.
Arrested by Henry II and her imprisonment began.
Transferred to England and imprisoned in Winchester Castle or Sarum Castle.
Death of Henry II's mistress, Rosamund Clifford.
Henry the Young King led another rebellion.
Death of Henry the Young King.
Summoned to Normandy by Henry II and stayed for six months.
Returned to England.
Death of Henry II; Richard I succeeded to the throne.
Richard I arrived in Portsmouth.
Truce agreement between Philip II and King John; marriage agreement between John's niece and Louis.
Arrived in Castile.
Crossed the Pyrenees with her granddaughter Blanche.
Assassination of Mercadier.
Health deteriorated; departed from Fontevraud to stop Arthur I from invading Poitiers.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Queen of France and England
keyAchievements.description: She was the Queen of King Louis VII of France, but after their divorce, she became the Queen of King Henry II of England.

keyAchievements.title: Queen Mother of Two English Kings
keyAchievements.description: She became the Queen Mother of Richard I and King John of England.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Patronage and Dissemination
keyAchievements.description: Patronized troubadours and literary figures, spreading the glamorous and refined courtly culture of southern France to France and England.

keyAchievements.title: Inheritance of Vast Estates
keyAchievements.description: Inherited the vast estates of the Duchy of Aquitaine and the County of Poitiers from her father.

keyAchievements.title: Regency Rule
keyAchievements.description: Ruled as regent when her son Richard I participated in the Third Crusade.

keyAchievements.title: Led Ransom Negotiations
keyAchievements.description: Traveled to the Holy Roman Empire to negotiate the ransom when Richard I was held captive.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Maritime Law
keyAchievements.description: Introduced maritime law to her lands in Oleron and to England.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Trade Customs
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in developing trade customs between the trading ports of the Crusader states and Constantinople.

keyAchievements.title: Support for Her Children
keyAchievements.description: Lived into the reign of her youngest son, John, continuing to support her sons.

keyAchievements.title: Arranged French Royal Marriage
keyAchievements.description: Selected Blanche of Castile as the bride for Prince Louis of France and arranged their marriage.

faq.question: What is Eleanor's estimated year of birth?
faq.answer: Based on records indicating she was 82 at the time of her death in 1204, she is estimated to have been born in 1122.

faq.question: Who was Eleanor married to?
faq.answer: She was the Queen of King Louis VII of France, but after their divorce, she became the Queen of King Henry II of England.

faq.question: Whom did Eleanor patronize?
faq.answer: Influenced by the Poitiers region, a center of 12th-century romantic literature, she patronized troubadours and literary figures.

faq.question: For how long was Eleanor imprisoned?
faq.answer: She was imprisoned from 1173 until the death of Henry II in 1189.

faq.question: Which of Eleanor's children became Kings of England?
faq.answer: Among the sons she had with Henry II, Richard I and King John became Kings of England.

faq.question: Is it true that Eleanor presided over 'Courts of Love'?
faq.answer: Historian Alison Weir concluded that this was a literary invention by Andreas Capellanus, and it is not mentioned in any sources other than Capellanus's book.

timeline.event: Proclaimed heiress of the House of Habsburg by the Pragmatic Sanction.
May 13, born as the eldest daughter of Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI and Elisabeth Christine of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel.
Fell in love with Francis Stephen of Lorraine.
February 12, married Francis Stephen of Lorraine.
The War of the Austrian Succession broke out after the death of Charles VI.
March 13, gave birth to son Joseph.
June 25, crowned Queen of Hungary.
September 11, addressed the Hungarian Diet and obtained war funds and troops.
Signed the Treaty of Dresden, recognizing Prussia's possession of Silesia.
October 4, successfully secured the imperial throne for her husband, Francis Stephen.
The War of the Austrian Succession ended with the signing of the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.
The Seven Years' War broke out between Prussia and Austria.
Russia withdrew from the Seven Years' War after the death of Elizabeth and the accession of Peter III.
Military organizations were consolidated under the Court Council of War, making it the supreme decision-making body.
Peace was made through the Treaty of Hubertusburg (Pressburg in some contexts), finalizing Prussia's possession of Silesia.
After the death of her husband Francis I, her son Joseph II was crowned Holy Roman Emperor.
Freedom of dress was implemented.
Approved the love marriage of her fourth daughter, Maria Christina.
Suppression of the Jesuits was implemented.
November 29, died.

keyAchievements.title: Domestic and Military Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the modernization of Austria by carrying out domestic reforms with the appointment of Haugwitz and military reforms led by Daun.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Appointed Kaunitz to forge an alliance with France, thereby ending the hostilities that had lasted since Maximilian I and achieving the Diplomatic Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Compulsory Education
keyAchievements.description: Established compulsory education by setting up elementary schools nationwide ahead of other countries, greatly raising the intellectual level of the populace.

keyAchievements.title: Prohibition and Crackdown on Prostitution
keyAchievements.description: Banned prostitution and had sex workers, pimps, and brokers arrested, whipped, and sent to labor camps for forced labor.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening of Military Power
keyAchievements.description: Significantly increased Austria's military strength by establishing a military administration committee, consolidating the Court Council of War, and adopting general conscription.

faq.question: Was Maria Theresa an empress?
faq.answer: Officially, she was the Empress Consort, not the Holy Roman Empress regnant, but since her husband's political power was minimal, she was practically recognized as the Empress by her contemporaries as the de facto ruler.

faq.question: What children did Maria Theresa have?
faq.answer: She had 16 children with Francis Stephen of Lorraine (5 sons and 11 daughters), including Joseph II, Leopold II, and Marie Antoinette.

faq.question: Did Maria Theresa reclaim Silesia?
faq.answer: She attempted to recapture Silesia through the War of the Austrian Succession and the Seven Years' War, but ultimately failed and recognized Prussia's possession of it.

faq.question: How did Maria Theresa become the ruler of the Habsburg dynasty?
faq.answer: Because her father Charles VI did not have a son, she was proclaimed the heiress of the House of Habsburg by the Pragmatic Sanction of 1713 and inherited his territories upon his death.

faq.question: What was Maria Theresa's education policy?
faq.answer: She established compulsory education by setting up elementary schools across the country before other nations, and distributed uniform textbooks to raise the intellectual level of the people.

timeline.event: Born in Folkestone, Kent, England.
Started studying medicine and liberal arts at Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge University.
Graduated from Cambridge University.
Went to study abroad at the University of Padua, Italy.
Returned home after completing his studies and opened a clinic in London.
Married Elizabeth Browne.
Elected as a Fellow of the Royal College of Physicians.
Appointed as a physician at St Bartholomew's Hospital.
Appointed as a lecturer in anatomy and surgery at the College.
Began giving lectures explaining his concepts and discoveries regarding the heart and blood.
Became Physician to King James I.
Became Physician to King Charles I.
Published 'Anatomical Exercise on the Motion of the Heart and Blood in Animals' (De Motu Cordis).
Wrote 'On the Generation of Animals' (De Generatione Animalium).
Elected President of the Royal College of Physicians but declined.
Died in London, England, on June 3.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Theory of Blood Circulation
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Anatomical Exercise on the Motion of the Heart and Blood in Animals', proposing the theory of blood circulation and proving it through modern scientific methods.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Embryology
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'On the Generation of Animals' and argued that all animals originate from an egg.

keyAchievements.title: Research on the Heart and Blood Vessels
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed that the valves between the atria and ventricles, and in the veins, allow blood to flow in only one direction, and discovered that the ventricles act as a pump.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Pulmonary Circulation
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the pulmonary circulation starting from the vena cava and leading to the arterial system.

faq.question: What was William Harvey's primary research method?
faq.answer: Harvey followed a modern scientific research framework by presenting and refuting the contradictions of existing theories, formulating new hypotheses, and verifying them through experiments.

faq.question: Was William Harvey's theory of blood circulation accepted at the time of publication?
faq.answer: His theory of blood circulation was not accepted at the time; even his mentor, Fabricius, allegedly criticized him.

faq.question: What were the main factors that influenced William Harvey's research?
faq.answer: His research was influenced by the public anatomy lectures at the University of Padua, his teacher Fabricius's experiment-based research methods, and Aristotle's microcosm theory.
